coredata.dc:title: Interface self-assembly preparation of multi-element doped carbon nanobowls with high electrocatalysis activity for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Xinyan
Song, Jiaxin
Zhao, Fengqi
Gao, Hongxu
Chen, Xinbing
Chen, Pei
An, Zhongwei
Chen, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Developing an efficient, stable and low cost oxygen reduction reaction electrocatalyst is desirable for fuel cells and metal-air batteries. Here, we have successfully prepared multi-element doped carbon nanobowls by simply mixing the porous carbon nanobowls and sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots in FeCl3 solution and subsequent high temperature treatment processes. Compared with the commercial Pt/C electrocatalyst, the multi-element doped carbon nanobowls display a comparable half-wave potential of 0.82 V, much larger limiting diffusion current density (0.4–0.8 V), better methanol-tolerance and higher long-term stability for the oxygen reduction reaction in alkaline media. The robust three-dimensional porous structure of carbon nanobowls and multiple active centers derived from Fe, N, S and O co-doping are responsible for the excellent performance. This work suggests that such multi-element doped carbon nanobowls can be a promising alternative for Pt-based catalysts in fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interface self-assembly
Multi-element doped carbon nanobowls
Electrocatalysis
Oxygen reduction reaction

Binding force of NS-dots, Fe ions and CBs
ORR performance of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical (A) SEM image, (B) TEM image, (C) HAADF-STEM image and corresponding element maps of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750.
(A) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, (B) the pore size distribution curves, (C) Raman spectra and (D) XRD patterns of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 and CBs.
(A) XPS full spectrum, (B) S2p, (C) Fe2p and (B) N1s fine XPS spectrum of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750.

(A) FTIR spectra of NS-dots and Fe-NS dots, (B) theexperimentalphenomena observed by adding a drop of the solution of puried NS-dots into the concentrated FeCl3 (left) and deionizedwater (right),(C) schematic representation of the interacting model of NS-dots, Fe3+ and CBs.

(A) CV curves of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 in N2/O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at scan rate of 50 mV s−1, (B) ORR polarization curves of the CBs, NS-dots, Fe-NS dots, NS-dots-750 and Fe-NS dots-750, NS-dots/CBs-750, Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 and Pt/C electrocatalysts at scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and a rotation rate of 1600 rpm, (C) ORR polarization curves of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 and Pt/C electrocatalysts before and after 5000 cycles. (D) LSV curves of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 at different rotation rates, (E) the Koutechy–Levich plots for Fe-NS dots/CBs-750. (F) RRDE measurements for Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 electrode. (B-F) were obtained in O2 -saturated 0.1 M KOH solution.

(A) The tolerance tested by current-time (i-t) chronoamperometric response toward methanol, and (B) LSV curves of Fe-NS dots/CBs750 before and after addition 0.1 mol L−1 NaSCN in electrolyte. All the data was obtained in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution.

head.ce:title: Interface self-assembly preparation of multi-element doped carbon nanobowls with high electrocatalysis activity for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Applied Surface and Colloid Chemistry (MOE), Shaanxi Key Laboratory for Advanced Energy Devices, Shaanxi Engineering Lab for Advanced Energy Technology, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi'an 710119, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Applied Surface and Colloid Chemistry (MOE)
Shaanxi Key Laboratory for Advanced Energy Devices
Shaanxi Engineering Lab for Advanced Energy Technology
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion, Xi'an Modern Chemistry Research Institute, 168 East Zhangba Road, Xi'an 710065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors are equal major contributors to this work (X. Shi and J. Song).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fe, N and S codoped carbon nanobowls exhibit excellent electrocatalytic activity, long-term stability and methanol-tolerance for the oxygen reduction reaction in alkaline media.

Developing an efficient, stable and low cost oxygen reduction reaction electrocatalyst is desirable for fuel cells and metal-air batteries. Here, we have successfully prepared multi-element doped carbon nanobowls by simply mixing the porous carbon nanobowls and sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots in FeCl3 solution and subsequent high temperature treatment processes. Compared with the commercial Pt/C electrocatalyst, the multi-element doped carbon nanobowls display a comparable half-wave potential of 0.82 V, much larger limiting diffusion current density (0.4–0.8 V), better methanol-tolerance and higher long-term stability for the oxygen reduction reaction in alkaline media. The robust three-dimensional porous structure of carbon nanobowls and multiple active centers derived from Fe, N, S and O co-doping are responsible for the excellent performance. This work suggests that such multi-element doped carbon nanobowls can be a promising alternative for Pt-based catalysts in fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) plays an important role in the energy conversion efficiency for the fuel cells and metal-air batteries [1]. Due to the sluggish kinetics of the ORR, platinum (Pt) is commonly used as the ORR electrocatalyst because of its high activity [2]. However, the high cost, and poor stability and low methanol resistance of Pt have extremely restricted its commercial application, and the development of non-noble metal electrocatalyst becomes a trend and necessity. To date, various carbon materials doped with nonmetallic elements (such as N, S, O, etc.) [3–5] and transition metallic elements (such as Fe, Co and Ni) [6] have shown excellent electrocatalytic activity for the ORR. Especially, iron-nitrogen-carbon (Fe-N/C) nanomaterials are considered as the most promising non-precious metal electrocatalysts to replace Pt-based electrocatalyst because of high activity and stability for the ORR in both alkaline and acidic medium [7–11].

It is generally accepted that the nitrogen-coordinated iron species of Fe-Nx (x = 2–4), as well as the non-metallic atoms (N, S, etc.) doped in the carbon framework, are the active sites for the ORR [12,13]. Thus, to further improve the electrocatalytic activity of Fe–N/C, more active sites are introduced into the Fe–N/C nanomaterials through additional doping of S or P heteroatoms [14,15]. In addition, constructing three-dimensional porous structures in Fe–N/C nanomaterials is also effective in the improvement of catalytic activity, due to the strong diffusion effect of ORR-relevant molecules in the pores on the reaction rate [16,17]. Consequently, various heteroatoms ternary-doped porous carbon materials have been prepared, such as Fe/N/S co-doped honeycomb carbon [18], Fe/N/S co-doped carbon with graphene-like structure [19], and Fe/N/S co-doped hollow carbon nanorods [20]. These nanomaterials have shown superior electrocatalytic activity for the ORR compared to Pt/C elecrtocatalyst. However, most of such nanomaterials are fabricated by pyrolyzing the mixtures of N, S or P-containing compounds and iron salts, which makes it difficult to well control the Fe–Nx active sites and effectively avoid the formation of inactive species such as iron oxide nanoparticles.

Graphitic carbon nitride possesses high nitrogen content (57.1 at%) but low electrical conductivity, resulting in an unsatisfactory electrocatalytic activity for the ORR, even it was integrated with conductive supports or doped with heteroatoms to modulate its electrical conductivity [21–24]. However, by means of its thermal decomposition into N doped carbon-based fragments beyond 700 °C [25], it can be used as sacrificial template and nitrogen sources to prepare ORR electrocatalyst, and the resultant nanomaterials show relatively high activity [26]. In this method, to obtain the uniformly mixed precursor, the bulk graphitic carbon nitride was generally pre-exfoliated to nanosheets, which is a process of time-consuming and low efficiency. Compared with the bulks and nanosheets, the quantum dots of graphitic carbon nitride have larger specific surface area, more edge effects and abundant superficial functional groups (–NH or –NH2), which favour to the assembly with other materials by hydrogen bonds, Van der Waals forces and so on [27]. Besides, preparation of heteroatoms doped graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots, such as S-doped graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (NS-dots), is very facile [28]. Therefore, NS-dots should be a suitable precursor to prepare N, S, Fe co-doped carbon-based electrocatalyst for the ORR.

In this work, we proposed to take full advantage of the high surface area and abundant pore structure of the carbon nanobowls (CBs) [29], and the high N and S doping level of NS-dots to develop a ORR electrocatalyst with high activity, where, the CBs was as the provider of porous structure but also supporter to avoid the aggregation of NS-dots and thus highly expose the active sites. A one-step preparation method was explored to combine CBs and NS-dots together and simultaneously introduce Fe3+ into the hybrids, by simply mixing NS-dots and CBs in FeCl3 solution and a subsequent heat treatment at N2 atmospheres. Compared with the commercial 20 wt% Pt/C, the prepared sample shows excellent ORR activity, featuring comparable half-wave potential (E 1/2) of 0.82 V, larger current density, much better cyclic stability and higher methanol tolerance in alkaline media.

Citric acid (CA, 99 wt%), thiourea (CN2H4S, 99 wt%), iron trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99 wt%), ethanol (C2H5OH, 99 wt%) were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Commercial 20 wt% Pt/C elelctrocatalyst was supplied by E-TEK, Inc.

NS-dots were prepared according to the literature with minor modification [28]. In a typical synthesis, 0.21 g of citric acid and 0.23 g thiourea were grinded in a mortar for 10 min, and then was heated in a stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C for 2 h. The product was dissolved with 40 mL water, and further purified with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to discard non-fluorescent deposit. The filtrate was collected for characterization and later use. The prepared NS-dots have similar morphology, structure and optical properties (Fig. S1) to that reported by literature [28].

In a typical preparation process, 14.48 mg FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in the collected filtrate containing NS-dots and stirred for 30 min, and then 50 mg CBs were added [29]. The mixture was treated with ultrasonic wave for 2 h and subsequently centrifugalized. The collected solids were washed with water and ethanol, respectively, followed by air-drying at 80 °C for 24 h. The dried powders were heated in a tubular furnace at 750 °C for 2 h under a N2 flow with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained sample was named as Fe-NS dots/CBs-750. For comparison, a series of controlled experiments were prepared with the same process, that is, the Fe-free sample of NS-dots/CBs-750, and the samples of NS-dots-750 and Fe-NS dots-750 without CBs, respectively.

Electrochemical experiments were carried out in a standard three-electrode system with computer-controlled CHI 760E electrochemical workstation equipped with rotating disk electrode (RDE) and rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) apparatus (Pine Company) at room temperature. A saturated calomel electrode and a carbon rod acted as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared by dropping 15 μL of the elelctrocatalyst ink on the polished glassy carbon disk electrode and then dried at room temperature. The elelctrocatalyst ink was obtained by mixing 10 mg of electrocatalyst and 5 mL of water/Nafion solution, and sonicating the mixture for 60 min. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) conducted on RDE system. LSV tests were conducted in 0.1 M KOH solution through varying the rotation speed varied from 500 to 2500 rpm with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The electron transfer number was calculated from the slope of the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots. The H2O2 yield and the electron transfer number during ORR were access with RRDE system at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and a rotation speed of 1600 rpm and calculated with the equations of X H2O2% = (2I ring/N)/(I disk + I ring/N) and n = 2X H2O2% + 4X H2O% = 4I disk/(I disk + I ring/N), respectively, in which I disk is the disk current density, I ring was the ring current density and N was the collection efficiency of the ring electrode. All potentials reported in this work are relative to the reversible hydrogen electrode (vs. RHE).

X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/max-rC), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F), scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM, FEI, Tecnai G2F20) equipped with X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), scan electron microscopy (SEM, SU-8020), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra), Raman spectrometer (In Via Reflex), and nitrogen physical adsorption-desorption instrument (ASAP 2020) were used to investigate the crystalline structure, morphology, chemical composition, surface area and pore structure of the samples. Thermal gravimetric (TG) analysis and was carried out with thermoanalyzer systems (Q600), and the functional groups were analyzed by using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet Is10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 was characterized with SEM and TEM. SEM image shows that this sample are composed of bowl-liked nanoparticles with size about of 300 nm (Fig. 1 A); TEM image (Fig. 1B) displays that the frameworks of these nanoparticles are not dense and possess porous microstructure, which is well in agreement with that of CBs (Fig. S2) [29]. As expect, no NS-dots is observed from SEM and TEM images, because they have decomposed into carbon-based fragments beyond 700 °C [25], as indicated by the marked mass loss in TG curve (TGAs) (Fig. S3A). In addition, in the cases without CBs (NS-dots and Fe-NS dots-750), the carbonized products have sheet-like morphology (Fig. S3B and 4B). To investigate the influence of CBs on the deposition status of the carbonized products of NS-dots and Fe ions, EDS mappings analysis was conducted. As shown in Fig. 1C, C, N, O, S and Fe are detected. C and O elements mainly belong to the CBs (Fig. S2D), N, S and Fe originate from the NS-dots and Fe ions, and their signals are completely superimposed with the elements of C and O, suggesting that the carbonized products of NS-dots and Fe ions uniformly load on the CBs.

To further study the effect of the carbonized products of NS-dots and Fe ions on the pore structure of CBs, nitrogen sorption measurement was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 A–B, Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 has similar isotherm curve and pore size distribution to that of CBs. Noticeably, the pore sizes of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 (1.1, 3.5 and 6.2 nm) are very close to that of CBs (1.3, 3.7 and 6.3 nm), demonstrating that it has similar pore structure to CBs. Combining EDS mappings and the slightly smaller pore size of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750, it can be deduced that the carbonized products of Fe-NS dots exist as a continuous ultrathin layer and uniformly cover the inner pore wall surfaces and the outer surface of the pores in CBs (Fig. S5). Under this condition, their loading amount should be low, which is confirmed by the estimation result (9.6 wt%) obtained from TG analysis (Fig. S3A). Because of the low loading amount, the specific surface area (739 m2 g−1) of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 slightly decreases relative to that of CBs (966 m2 g−1). The hierarchical pore structure and relatively high surface area of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 facilitate the mass transport during the electrochemical reaction.

The structural features of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 were further characterized by Raman (Fig. 2C) and XRD spectra (Fig. 2D). The typical D and G bands in Raman spectra are clearly observed. Compared with the CBs, the D-band of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 shifts only 2 cm−1, which is within a reasonable error range and can be ignored; the G-band shifts 3 cm−1 to lower wave numbers due to the nitrogen doping [30]. Importantly, the ratio of the intensity of D to G band is about of 0.83 in both case of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 and the CBs, proving that the amorphous carbon and graphitized carbon coexist in these two samples. The amorphous carbon means the presence of abundant defects that are beneficial for O2 adsorption, and the graphitized carbon indicates good electrical conductivity that allows a fast electron transport during ORR. Both are favorable to the activity of electrocatalyst [31]. XRD spectrum of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 exhibits two characteristic graphitic peaks at around 23° and 44°, assigned to (0 0 2) and (1 0 1), respectively, and no any diffraction peaks corresponding to Fe, FeOx or FeCx are detected, excluding the presence of any large crystalline particles of Fe-containing species. However, the XRD spectrum of the Fe-NS dots-750 without CBs shows the peaks of Fe3O4 (Fig. S4A), in consistent with TEM observation (Fig. S4C). This phenomenon in turn testifies the crucial role of CBs in inhibiting the agglomeration of NS-dots and Fe.

XPS measurements were conducted to investigate the chemical states of N, S and Fe as well as their local environment, which are important to the ORR performances of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 A) reveals the presence of C, O, N, S and Fe. The O element mainly inherits from the CBs [29], and exists in the form of OH, CO and COC functional groups as shown the FTIR spectrum in Fig. S6. It has been proposed that these O species can create a synergistic coupling effect with other doped heteroatom such as N and S for enhancing the catalytic performance for the ORR [5,32–35]. In addition, these O species offer hydrophilicity to the carbon-based electrocatalyst, ensuring the sufficient contact of oxygen dissolved in the electrolyte with the electrocatalyst in the electrochemical reaction [36,37]. It is noted that the contents of N, S and Fe are 2.15, 0.19 and 0.16 at.%, respectively, and are essentially consistent with the EDS results (5.15, 0.38 and 0.23 at.%, Fig. S7), meaning N, S and Fe species uniformly distribute in the CBs. The high-resolution S2p spectrum (Fig. 3B) shows the peaks of p1/2 and p3/2 at binding energy of 163.4 and 164.3 eV which attribute to CSC bonds, and the ones at 166.0–170 eV are associated with the oxidized-S species that are chemically inactive for the ORR [38]. In addition, the S2− and S n
2− species originating from Fe1
−x S were not detected [39], inferring that Fe probably coordinated with nitrogen to form the well-distributed ORR active sites of FeNx. The XPS Fe2p (Fig. 3C) could be deconvoluted into five peaks, including the Fe(III) 2p1/2 peak at 725.6 eV, the Fe(II) 2p1/2 peak at 723.4 eV, the Fe(III) 2p3/2 peak at 714.6 eV, the Fe(II) 2p3/2 peak at 710.7 eV, and the satellite peak at 719.6 eV [40,41], respectively, suggesting the coexistence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in this sample. The deconvoluted N 1s spectra show four peaks (Fig. 3D), ascribing to the pyridinic-N/Fe-Nx (398.1 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.9 eV), graphitic-N (400.9 eV), and oxidized-N (405.1 eV) species, respectively [42,43]. The graphitic-N, pyridinic-N and Fe-Nx are responsible for the high ORR activity [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding force of NS-dots, Fe ions and CBs
ce:section.ce:para: To controllably prepare Fe-NS dots/CBs-750, it is necessary to clarify the binding force among NS-dots, Fe3+ and CBs. As well known, the interface bonding model, such as electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals force and π-π stacking, plays an important role in the loading amount, uniform dispersion and even the catalysis activity [46]. Considering the carbon colloidal nanoparticles usually have negative charge, we investigated the assembly process of NS-dots, Fe3+ and CBs from the angle of electrostatic interaction. Zeta potential measurements show that the NS-dots and CBs have negative charges of −30.4 and −24.0 mV, respectively, indicating that their binding process is driven mainly by π-π stacking not electrostatic attraction [47]. However, after mixing with 1.34 μM of FeCl3 solution, the zeta potential of NS-dots changed from negative to positive and was of +7.6 mV due to adsorption of Fe3+. To investigate the adsorption mechanism of Fe3+, the NS-dots were dialyzed with the membrane of cutoff 1000 Da for 24 h to remove small molecules, and then were collected by rotary evaporation and characterized by FTIR. From the FTIR spectrum of NS-dots (Fig. 4 A), CN stretching vibration of SCN− ions is observed at 2061 cm−1 as a sharp absorption peak [48], besides the adsorption peaks of OH/NH2 (3435, 3188 cm−1), CO (1700 cm−1), CC/CN (1597–1450 cm−1), CN/COO (1400–1300 cm−1), CS/CO (1180–1080 cm−1) and tri-s-triazine rings (800 cm−1) [49]. This is an indication of SCN− adsorbing on the surface of NS-dots. To further prove it, a drop of the purified NS-dots solution was added into 1 M of FeCl3 solution. As expected, the bloody red, the characteristic color of [Fe(SCN)n]3−n ions, is clearly observed (Fig. 4B). Based on these results, it can refer that Fe3+ bonds together with NS-dots through the bridge of SCN− ions, and simultaneously changes the zeta potential of NS-dots. Consequently, the Fe-NS dots deposited on the CBs by electrostatic attraction, just as shown in Fig. 4C. The subsequent heat treatment initiates many complex chemical reactions, including the decomposition of NS-dots and [Fe(SCN)n]3−n ions to form gas molecules and the carbides. In these elaborate chemical process, N, S and Fe atoms covalently bonded with the CBs uniformly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR performance of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 for the ORR, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte was conducted (Fig. 5 A). Relative to the smooth CV curve obtained from the N2-saturated electrolyte (Fig. 5A), the CV curve in O2-saturated electrolyte shows a cathodic peak at 0.78 V, indicating the electrocatalytic activity of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 for the ORR in alkaline media.

The ORR performance of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 was further measured with the RDE using LSV technique. For comparison, the ORR activities of the CBs, NS-dots, Fe-NS dots, NS-dots-750, Fe-NS dots-750, NS-dots/CBs-750 and Pt/C electrocatalyst were also investigated under the same experimental conditions (Fig. 5B). Unsurprised, whether the presence of Fe or not, whether heat treatment or not, NS-dots, Fe-NS dots, NS-dots-750 and Fe-NS dots-750 exhibit poor ORR catalytic activity. For the NS-dots and Fe-NS dots, their poor activity is mainly ascribed to lack of catalysis active sites, because many atoms of N, S and Fe exist as the functional groups or [Fe(SCN)n]3−n ions (Fig. 4), which are inactive species for the ORR. After carbonizing the NS-dots and Fe-NS dots at 750 °C, the nitrogen atoms have incorporated into the carbonized matrix and formed the active sites for ORR, but the NS-dots-750 and Fe-NS dots-750 still show the low ORR activity, chiefly attributing to the serious agglomeration (Fig. S3) (which is unfavorable to mass transport) and the formation of inactive species Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. S4A). Relative to these dots, the CBs do not contain the N and Fe atoms, while display a certain degree of catalysis activity for the ORR, probably due to the active sites stemming from the edge defects [50] and the O-containing species [51]. But the E 1/2 value and the limited diffusion current density in the case of CBs are only 0.76 eV and 3 mA cm−2 (at 0.2 V), which are far inferior than that of Pt/C, indicating such active sites have a lower catalysis efficiency. After loading NS-dots on the CBs and subsequently heat treatment, the resultant sample of NS dots/CBs-750 exhibits higher catalytic activity for the ORR than that of CBs, with the aid of the newly formed active sites due to N and S doping. When Fe NS-dots were composed together with CBs and carbonized at 750 °C, the ORR catalytic activity is further remarkably improved, thanking for the newly formed Fe-Nx active sites besides that of N and S doping. The Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 shows a comparable E 1/2 value (0.82 V) to the commercial 20 wt% Pt/C (0.84 V) and notably higher diffusion current density over a large potential range (0.2–0.8 V). The high current density benefits from the multiple active sites, but also from the efficient electrolyte/reactant diffusion guaranteed by the larger surface area, abundant pore structure and the surface hydrophilicity originating from the O-containing functional groups [36,37,52]. In addition, the E 1/2 value (0.82 V) is also more positive than that of the other carbon-based electrocatalysts, such as various carbon-based quantum dots and the heteroatom doped carbon nanomaterials (Table S1). These results indicate that Fe, N and S co-doping into the carbon matrix, the high specific surface area and hierarchical pore structure of the CBs effectively enhance the ORR performance.

The long-term stability of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 was assessed by cycling the electrode between 0 and 1.2 V at 10 mV s−1 under O2-saturated 0.1 m KOH electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 5C, the E 1/2 only shifts negatively by 1 mV after 5000 continuous cycles, which is much smaller than that (35 mV) of Pt/C, implying good durability of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750.

The ORR reaction kinetics at the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 was further investigated. Normally, the limiting diffusion current increases with the rotation speed due to the thinned diffusion layer (Fig. 5D). The transferred electron number (n) per oxygen molecule involved in the ORR process was calculated with Koutecky–Levich equation, which was to be ca. 3.87 in the potential range of 0.2–0.6 V (Fig. 5E), suggesting an approximate four-electron pathway. A RRDE test was conducted to explore the electron transfer mechanism and hydrogen peroxide yields. As demonstrated in Fig. 5F, the ring current originating from the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide ions (HO2−) is low. The calculated percentage of HO2− is below 13% over the potential range from 0.2 to 0.8 V, giving an electron transfer number ∼3.92. This is agreement with the results obtained from the Koutecky–Levich plots, again confirming a nearly 4e - pathway for the ORR catalyzed by the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750.

In addition, the tolerance of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 to methanol was studied by chronoamperometry. As demonstrated in Fig. 6A, after adding methanol into the O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte to the concentration of 1 M, the ORR current for Pt/C electrocatalyst shows a drastic sharp surge and cannot be recovered to the initial level. By contrast, the current level of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 has no obvious change, indicating its excellent methanol-tolerance.

To clarify the active species of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750, an electrochemical poisoning test was carried out using a 0.1 M NaSCN alkaline aqueous solution, based on the common knowledge of SCN- ions blocking the Fe–Nx species that are highly active for the ORR. As shown in Fig. 6 B, the E onset and E 1/2 of Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 negatively shift of 62 and 60 mV, respectively. This phenomenon is agreement with the poisoned effect observed in cases of other Fe–Nx–C-based electrocatalysts [53,54], strongly indicating that the Fe–Nx species exist in the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 electrocatalyst and play an important role in catalyzing ORR [55]. In fact, it has been elucidated that the trace Fe (0.2 at.%) is favorable to the formation of highly active Fe-N4 sites and thus improve the catalytic activity of the N-doped carbon nanomaterials [56], which is in good consistent with the results in this work. On the other hand, although the electrocatalytic activity reduced due to the poisoned Fe–Nx species, the E 1/2 is still up to 0.76 V and much higher than that of the CBs, suggesting the presence of the other active sites derived from N and S doping. Thus, it can conclude that the synergetic effect of these active sites is responsible for the superior electrocatalytic performance of the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750.

In summary, we developed an interfacial self-assembly method to prepare carbon-based electrocatalyst for the ORR. By simply mixing porous CBs, and NS-dots into FeCl3 solution, NS-dots and Fe3+ ions were nailed on the surface of CBs, driven by the electrostatic attraction and coordination bonding. After carbonization, a N, S, O, Fe multiple doped carbon nanobowls (Fe-NS dots/CBs-750) with porous structure were successfully prepared. Benefited from the synergistic effect of the multiple active sites, as well as the enriched porosity and high surface area, the optimized Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 revealed excellent ORR activity, featuring high E 1/2 (0.82 V), large limiting current density (7 mA cm−2 at 0.2 V), and a four electron-dominated transfer mechanism. Moreover, the Fe-NS dots/CBs-750 showed better long-term stability and methanol tolerance than that commercial Pt/C, demonstrating its potential application in fuel cells and metal–air batteries as alternative Pt electrocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Science Foundation Committee of China (51873100, 51773112), Program for Science & Technology Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (2018TD-030), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (GK201801001, GK201703030), and National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (201710718018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.08.103.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
